description:
You are acting as a DevSecOps engineer who must transform an existing CSV that lists policy-violation events into a trimmed, tab-separated feed that is ready to be ingested by an internal SIEM.

    Initial data set (already present on disk):
    1. /home/user/company_logs/policy_violations.csv  
       ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
       │date,user,policy,severity,action,status                                      │
       │2023-06-01,alice,SSH Root Login,Critical,Alert,OPEN                          │
       │2023-06-02,bob,Password Reuse,High,Block,CLOSED                              │
       │2023-06-03,carol,Public S3 Bucket,Medium,Alert,OPEN                          │
       │2023-06-03,dave,Unencrypted S3 Bucket,High,Alert,OPEN                        │
       │2023-06-04,erin,Insecure Protocol,Critical,Block,CLOSED                      │
       │2023-06-05,frank,Outdated SSL Cert,Medium,Alert,OPEN                         │
       │2023-06-06,grace,Open Security Group,High,Alert,OPEN                         │
       │2023-06-07,heidi,Missing MFA,Critical,Alert,CLOSED                           │
       └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

    2. /home/user/company_logs/active_employees.txt  
       ┌────────────────────┐
       │alice               │
       │carol               │
       │dave                │
       │frank               │
       │grace               │
       │mallory             │
       └────────────────────┘

    Your tasks (5-10 shell commands will be enough):
    A. Using only standard *text-processing* utilities available on a minimal Linux install (e.g. cut, paste, awk, grep, sort, comm, join; no Python or Perl), create the output directory:
       /home/user/output/

    B. From policy_violations.csv, keep only the rows
       • whose “status” column equals the literal string OPEN, **and**  
       • whose “user” value is **present** in active_employees.txt.

    C. For those qualifying rows, output exactly four columns in the following order:
       date, user, policy, severity  
       (drop action and status completely).  
       The final artefact must be a tab-separated values file named
       /home/user/output/filtered_violations.tsv

       Output-file format requirements that the automated tests will verify:
       1. The file must begin with a header row that reads literally:  
          date<TAB>user<TAB>policy<TAB>severity
       2. Each subsequent data line must contain exactly three TAB characters.  
       3. After the last data line, insert a single blank line, then append one summary line (no TABs) with the exact wording:  
          # Total OPEN active violations: N  
          where N is the count of data lines you produced.
       4. The file must end with a single trailing newline (U+000A).

    D. Create a plain-text processing log file at
       /home/user/output/processing.log  
       containing exactly one line:
       filtered_violations.tsv generated successfully

    E. Ensure both final files are group- and world-readable (chmod 644 is sufficient).

    Do **not** hard-code the number of violations; derive it programmatically from the filtered data so the summary line is correct even if the source data changes.

    Hint guidance (do NOT output these literal commands):  
    • Use grep or awk to discard CLOSED rows.  
    • Use join or grep -F -f to cross-reference against active_employees.txt.  
    • Use cut or awk to emit the required columns, then redirect to the .tsv file.  
    • Use wc -l or awk NR to compute the record count for the summary line.  

    Once you believe everything is complete, list the contents of /home/user/output and show the first 10 lines of filtered_violations.tsv so a reviewer can quickly verify correctness.
truth:
The harness will pre-populate the workspace with the following files *exactly as shown*:

    1. /home/user/company_logs/policy_violations.csv  
       date,user,policy,severity,action,status
       2023-06-01,alice,SSH Root Login,Critical,Alert,OPEN
       2023-06-02,bob,Password Reuse,High,Block,CLOSED
       2023-06-03,carol,Public S3 Bucket,Medium,Alert,OPEN
       2023-06-03,dave,Unencrypted S3 Bucket,High,Alert,OPEN
       2023-06-04,erin,Insecure Protocol,Critical,Block,CLOSED
       2023-06-05,frank,Outdated SSL Cert,Medium,Alert,OPEN
       2023-06-06,grace,Open Security Group,High,Alert,OPEN
       2023-06-07,heidi,Missing MFA,Critical,Alert,CLOSED

    2. /home/user/company_logs/active_employees.txt  
       alice
       carol
       dave
       frank
       grace
       mallory

    After the agent finishes, the verifier will expect **both** of the following files to exist with this exact content (including newlines):

    1. /home/user/output/filtered_violations.tsv  
       date\tuser\tpolicy\tseverity\n
       2023-06-01\talice\tSSH Root Login\tCritical\n
       2023-06-03\tcarol\tPublic S3 Bucket\tMedium\n
       2023-06-03\tdave\tUnencrypted S3 Bucket\tHigh\n
       2023-06-05\tfrank\tOutdated SSL Cert\tMedium\n
       2023-06-06\tgrace\tOpen Security Group\tHigh\n
       \n
       # Total OPEN active violations: 5\n

       (That is 8 physical lines, with exactly one blank line before the summary.)

    2. /home/user/output/processing.log  
       filtered_violations.tsv generated successfully\n

    Permissions:
      Both files must have mode 0644 (rw-r--r--). The verifier will *not* run with sudo, so the agent has to set permissions accordingly.

    Directory /home/user/output/ must exist by the end of the task.
name: task_000000_9ab9f0d7
